Hydroxyurea Initiation Study — Data Preparation Report

Ahodwo Facility Patient Records

22 April 2026

1 Overview

This document describes every transformation applied to the raw patient dataset and provides visual summaries of the final cleaned data. It is intended to accompany the analysis and serve as an audit trail of all data preparation decisions made before the prediction model was built.

The goal of the model is to predict which sickle cell disease patients will be initiated on Hydroxyurea, using their clinical history and longitudinal laboratory results.

2 Raw Data — What We Started With

The raw Excel file (Append1 sheet) contained 22,052 rows and 39 columns before any processing.

Total rows in raw file 22,052
Total columns 39
Sheet name Append1

3 Step-by-Step Manipulations

3.1 Step 1 — Load Data

The file was read directly from Excel using readxl::read_excel() targeting the Append1 sheet. This preserves original column names (spaces, brackets, special characters) exactly as they appear in the source file, which are then cleaned in Step 3.

3.2 Step 2 — Drop Phantom Rows

The bottom 5,204 rows of the file contained no patient information — only a Hu Start flag. These are almost certainly a formatting artefact from the data export (a secondary summary table appended to the main records). Including them would skew the outcome balance.

3.3 Step 3 — Standardise Column Names

All 16 columns with messy names (spaces, brackets, slashes, dots) were renamed to clean snake_case equivalents. Examples:

Original name Renamed to
Hu Start(0/1) Hu_Start
hgb(g/dl) hgb
rbc *10^12 rbc
Hb Genotype hb_genotype
form.toxicity toxicity
history.num_pain_events num_pain_events
history.experienced_pain experienced_pain

Columns that were already clean (e.g. id_hu_study_number, platelet_count) were left as-is.

3.4 Step 4 — Drop Columns That Should Never Be Used

16 columns were removed:

Column Reason
hu_start_date Target leakage — directly encodes the outcome
patient_full_name PII — must never enter a model
patient_med_record_num PII — must never enter a model
history.pain_history_notes 98.8% empty; free text, not structured
Date Patient Registration 92.9% empty; too sparse to use
Index Bare row index, no clinical meaning
Column1 Unnamed column, no clinical meaning
Column2 Unnamed column, no clinical meaning
form.blood_test_results.select_labs Form selector field, no clinical value
anc (SI Unit) / (Conv. Unit) Duplicate unit variants of anc (keep primary)
arc (SI unit) / (Conv. unit) Duplicate unit variants of arc (keep primary)
wbc (SI Unit) 10^9/L Duplicate unit variant of wbc (keep primary)
hct (SI unit) / hct(%) Duplicate unit variants of hct (keep primary)

3.5 Step 5 — Filter to Laboratory Visits Only

The dataset contains three visit types. Only Lab visits carry blood test results — the others were excluded.

3.6 Step 6 — Remove Duplicates

After filtering to Lab visits, exact duplicate rows were removed using duplicated() across all columns simultaneously. A patient should not appear twice with identical information on the same date.

3.7 Step 7 — Convert and Standardise Data Types

Column(s) Conversion applied
Lab_Visit_Date, Clinic_Visit_Date, dob Excel serial numbers → proper Date objects (origin: 30 Dec 1899)
hgb, anc, arc, platelet_count, rbc, mcv, hct, wbc Text/mixed → numeric; non-convertible entries become NA
gender Lowercased + trimmed; missing values labelled "unknown" → factor
hb_genotype Lowercased + trimmed → factor with levels: ss, sc, sbo, dont_know
toxicity Cast to factor with levels: none, low_anc, low_hb, low_platelet_count, other
age Truncated to whole integer years

3.8 Step 8 — Handle Missing Lab Values

Missing values in all eight lab columns were replaced with the column median computed from the full dataset.

Important note: This imputation uses all available rows because R’s role here is data preparation and descriptive reporting only — not model training. In Python, imputation will be recomputed on training rows only and then applied to the test set separately to prevent any data leakage across the train/test split.

3.9 Step 9 — Feature Engineering

Three new sets of features were created:

Feature Description
age_group "Paediatric" (age < 18) or "Adult" (age ≥ 18)
days_in_care Days elapsed since that patient’s first lab visit
hgb_delta Difference between current Hgb and the patient’s baseline (first visit) Hgb
hgb_prev Hgb value from the patient’s previous visit
wbc_prev WBC value from the patient’s previous visit
anc_prev ANC value from the patient’s previous visit

For a patient’s first visit, hgb_prev, wbc_prev, and anc_prev are NA (no prior visit exists). These were filled with the column median.

4 Final Dataset — Summary

Total rows (lab visits) 10,632
Total columns 29
Unique patients 1,556
Average visits per patient 6.8
Engineered features added 6

5 Visualisations of the Final Clean Data

5.1 Outcome Distribution — Hu_Start

The outcome is imbalanced — approximately 78% of visits are from patients on Hydroxyurea. This will be addressed during modelling with appropriate class balancing techniques.

5.2 Age Distribution

5.3 Gender Distribution

7 Final Column Inventory

Column Type
Case_ID character
Hu_Start factor
id_hu_study_number character
Facility_name character
Clinic_Visit_Date character
Lab_Visit_Date character
Visit.type character
Diagnosis.Method.Formatted character
hb_genotype factor
gender factor
age integer
dob character
hgb numeric
anc numeric
arc numeric
platelet_count integer
wbc numeric
rbc numeric
mcv numeric
hct numeric
toxicity factor
experienced_pain logical
num_pain_events logical
age_group factor
days_in_care numeric
hgb_delta numeric
hgb_prev numeric
wbc_prev numeric
anc_prev numeric

Report generated automatically from data_cleaning.r output. Working dataset: ML/data/hu_clean_data.csv
